purpose: Prioritised framework order for OpenCompliance depth work, combining core assurance frameworks with the next wave of public AI governance and AI security standards relevant to legal-tech deployer style environments.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Still the strongest customer-facing security management baseline for SaaS diligence and procurement.
frameworks.nextAction: Continue private seed decomposition, promote the remaining availability and vulnerability-management slices, and keep blocker-aware exact-anchor review explicit.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Directly relevant to US buyer assurance, trust-center narratives, and recurring security questionnaires.
frameworks.nextAction: Continue private seed decomposition and publish only blocker-aware narrow control mappings until licensed criterion review exists.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Directly relevant to UK and EU personal-data handling, customer diligence, and AI systems that process personal data.
frameworks.nextAction: Deepen reviewed article-level coverage around monitoring, transparency, individual rights, and AI-adjacent accountability.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Important for Australian government and regulated-enterprise pathways, especially for a legal-tech deployer pushing into Australia.
frameworks.nextAction: Deepen public exact-anchor coverage for monitoring, incident handling, and boundary controls while keeping hosted-shared-responsibility assumptions explicit.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Most important binding AI governance regime for EU-facing deployments and for any provable AI transparency or oversight story.
frameworks.nextAction: Keep the current documentary corridor honest and deepen into typed technical-documentation, logging, and post-market monitoring slices.

frameworks.framework: UK ICO AI and data protection guidance
frameworks.operatorBenefit: Highly relevant where AI use intersects with personal data, transparency, and UK-regulator expectations.
frameworks.nextAction: Map the current AI context, risk, oversight, and disclosure controls to guidance-grade anchors and keep them clearly separate from statutory GDPR anchors.

frameworks.framework: UK AI Cyber Security Code of Practice
frameworks.operatorBenefit: Best near-term public baseline for securing AI systems used or operated by UK software vendors.
frameworks.nextAction: Use it to shape the next planned AI security control family around AI threat modelling, monitoring, and secure lifecycle operations.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Strong voluntary structure for operational AI governance, especially when the company wants a rigorous internal control language without waiting for certification.
frameworks.nextAction: Keep using it as the public exact-anchor backbone for the current AI-governance corridor and newer ISO crosswalk candidates.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Directly relevant for generative-AI deployments and complements the core NIST AI RMF with GenAI-specific risk treatment.
frameworks.nextAction: Promote it from family-proxy planning into reviewed anchors for disclosure, oversight, and post-deployment monitoring.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Most obvious management-system-style target for organisations that want AI governance to look certifiable and auditable.
frameworks.nextAction: Keep publishing only crosswalk-derived candidate anchors until licensed clause review exists.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Useful for impact-assessment depth and for turning AI risk work into a more explicit, reviewable artifact chain.
frameworks.nextAction: Use it to shape impact-assessment-oriented claim types and candidate exact-anchor plans.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Relevant for Australian deployments and especially useful because it is deployer-oriented and openly structured as ten guardrails.
frameworks.nextAction: Treat it as the Australian public-anchor bridge for context, risk, oversight, monitoring, and disclosure controls.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Strong European baseline for AI cybersecurity, especially useful if a legal-tech deployer wants a public AI security story beyond governance prose.
frameworks.nextAction: Use it to shape the first non-fixture AI security controls before attempting exact-anchor publication.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Directly relevant to OpenCompliance itself because it frames continuous auditing-based conformity assessment for dynamic AI systems.
frameworks.nextAction: Use it as a design input for the transparency, witness, and continuous-verification plane rather than as a product compliance target only.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Useful for risk-management language and audit conversations, but not as primary as 42001 or 42005 for the current corridor.
frameworks.nextAction: Keep it as a candidate exact-anchor layer behind NIST AI RMF and 42001 until licensed review exists.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: High-value if a legal-tech deployer wants to prove GenAI-aware secure development practices rather than only governance documentation.
frameworks.nextAction: Use it to shape future secure-development and model-lifecycle controls, not the current documentary AI-governance corridor.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Important if a legal-tech deployer ever trains, fine-tunes, or meaningfully distributes general-purpose models instead of only deploying third-party models.
frameworks.nextAction: Keep it in the review layer but avoid over-prioritising it unless the operating model changes toward GPAI provider responsibilities.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Potentially important for dual-use or foundation-model misuse risk, but still draft and less immediately relevant for ordinary deployer controls.
frameworks.nextAction: Track the draft and use it to shape future foundation-model misuse controls only if the operator expands into that risk class.

frameworks.operatorBenefit: Relevant to the audit and certification ecosystem around AI management systems, but not a direct first-order operator control framework.
frameworks.nextAction: Track it as ecosystem infrastructure for the certification layer rather than as an immediate control-mapping target.
